turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: What was to do or to make in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hacer. Good. How would you say it does or it makes?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hace. Good. Now hace, you can use like ago. Like in English when you say two weeks ago, you can say it makes two weeks in Spanish, and you get that meaning. So week is semana. Two is - do you remember two?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Dos. So two weeks ago, you will say it makes two weeks:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hace dos semanas. I saw him two weeks ago:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Lo vi hace dos semanas. Good. To respond is responder. Responder. How would you say I responded?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Respondí. How would you say I responded to her?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Le respondí. I responded to her. I responded to her seven weeks ago. Do you remember how to say seven? Related to September:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Siete. Good. So I responded to her seven weeks ago:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Le respondí hace siete semanas. Now in Spanish, you can say I responded her it. For example, I responded to her letter or I responded to her mail. Instead of saying it this way, to her mail, in Spanish you can just say I responded him the mail. I responded her the mail. Or if you know what you are talking about, I responded him it. I responded her it. This is because, and something we’ll look at again later, Spanish does not use possessive words as much as English does. So, it would be much more natural in Spanish, I responded her the mail. I responded him the, which isn’t mail, instead of I responded to her mail. So how would you say I responded her it great English, but it’s perfect Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Se lo respondí. So the se is actually le, to her. The lo is el mail and you get se lo respondí. I responded to her the mail. Good. Month is mes. Mes. M-e-s.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: It’s a masculine word, so how would you say the month?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good, and how would you say a month or one month?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Un mes. So un mes can be a month or one month. So how would you say I responded it to her one month ago? I responded to her mail, I responded her it one month ago:

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Se lo respondí hace un mes.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Se lo respondí hace un mes. Good. So we have respondí. I responded. How would you say he, she, it, you (formal) responded?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Respondió. And we responded:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Respondimos. So respondí, respondió, respondimos. Let’s make a quick recap with an - ar verb. How would you say discrimination?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Again?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: In English we say discrimination or descrimination?

turns.speaker: student
expected_answers: Oh, it’s with an i, yeah.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: It’s with an i, right?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Discriminación. To discriminate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Discriminar. I discriminated:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Discriminé. He, or she, or it, you (formal) discriminated:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. And we discriminated:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. So we have two more people to learn for the point in the past. They and you informal. Let’s begin with you informal. For you informal, in the point in the past we have - aste for - ar verbs and - iste for -er/-ir verbs. - aste for - ar verbs and - iste for -er/-ir verbs. So how would you say you spoke, speaking informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablaste. And you ate, speaking informally:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Comiste. Did you eat?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Comiste?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ No comiste?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ No lo comiste? Good. What was to leave or to go out?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salir. How would you say did you go out? Did you leave?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Saliste? Good. is anoche. Anoche.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Now if you translate tonight directly, what you will come up with is anoche. So you must be careful not to get confused here. How do you say tonight in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esta noche anoche is last night, not tonight. So how would you say did you go out last night (speaking informally)?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Saliste anoche?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ No saliste anoche? What was to live?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Vivir. And how do you say where?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Donde. Where did you live?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Dónde viviste? Where were you living?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Dónde vivías? So in many cases both line and point are perfectly fine. ¿ Dónde viviste? ¿ Dónde vivías? All that means is that the speaker has two different ideas in their mind. Maybe you go abroad to study and I’ll tell you ¿ dónde viviste? Or maybe I’ll tell you ¿ dónde vivías? ¿Dónde viviste? Where did you live? And this idea in my mind which is this closed amount of time. Where were you living? just means the idea in my mind is an ongoing idea of time. So many times it is completely interchangeable. What was to wait?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esperar. Esperar. So for you informal, we have -aste for the point in the past. So how would you say you waited, speaking informally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Esperaste. Good. Did you wait for me?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: ¿ Me esperaste?:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Por qúe no me esperaste? What was to arrive?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Llegar. Good. How do you say when did you arrive? When did you arrive? (informally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. ¿ Cuándo llegaste? This is an example of when you would need the point, because you probably wouldn’t want to say, no? So here’s very strongly the point in when were you arriving time. When did you arrive? ¿ Cuándo llegaste? So we have -aste and -iste for you informal. For they, we have -aron for -ar verbs and - ieron for -er/-ir verbs. So, how would you say they spoke? They spoke:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablaron. They waited:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. They prepared it:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Lo prepararon. Good. We have -aron for -ar verbs and -ieron, i-e-r-o-n, for -er/-ir verbs. How would you say they ate?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comieron. They left, they went out:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Salieron. They sold them:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Good. Los vendieron with hablar and comer which we might want to use as our hooks. So we’ll begin with hablar. How do you say I spoke?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablé. He spoke:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Habló. We spoke:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablamos. You spoke (informal):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablaste. And they spoke:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablaron. Good. Hablé, habló, hablamos, hablaste, hablaron. Now let’s do the same for comer, so for -er/-ir verbs. How do you say I ate?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comí. He, she, it, you (formal) ate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comió. We ate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: That’s the present.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comimos. Good. You ate (informally):

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comiste. And they ate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comieron -er/-ir verbs in the present, we had / e / popping up everywhere. Vive, viven, vives and just one / i /. Just one / i / sound. Just one i popping up, vivimos. But everything else was / e / for -er/-ir verbs in the present, but in the past for -er/-ir verbs we have i dominating the stage. We have the i, / i / sound everywhere and this time the letter e, the / e / sound, occupying only one space. Popping up in only one place which is -ieron. Comí, comió, comiste, comimos, comieron which is the exact inverse of what happens in the present tense. We, can see also a logic that crosses over between the - ar group and the -er/-ir group. For example, we have I spoke, how is it?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablé. And he spoke:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Habló. We saw the -er/-ir in this past point has a special affiliation to the / i / sound, the i, the letter i. So whilst we have hablé for -ar verbs, for -er/-ir verbs what do we have? How would we say I ate?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comí. And whilst we have habló for - ar verbs, what do we have for -er/-ir verbs? How would you say he ate?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comió -ar version, but with that important / i / popping up again. We have, for we spoke, what do we have?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablamos. And for we ate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comimos. That important / i /, the i, the / i / sound again. For you (informal) spoke, what do we have?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablaste. But you ate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comiste. You remembered the importance of that i. Comiste. They spoke:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Hablaron. And they ate:

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Comieron. Again that important i and that e getting just one small look in, in that dot in the past to remind us that this is -er/-ir group, not just - ir. Just as what happened the other way around in the present. Good.
